coredata.dc:title: AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti nanocomposite coatings for combating implant-associated infections by photodynamic /photothermal therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Huo, Dongliang
Liu, Ting
Huang, Kangkang
Que, Changhui
Jiang, Shuoyan
Yang, Yuxia
Tan, Shaozao
Huang, Langhuan
coredata.dc:description: Biofilm-mediated implant-associated infections are one of the most serious complications of implantation surgery, posing a grave threat to patient well-being. Effectively addressing bacterial infections is crucial for the success of implantation procedures. In this study, we prepared a bismuth sulfide silver@carbon quantum dot composite coating (AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti) on a medical titanium surface by surface engineering design to treat implant-associated infections. The photocatalytic/photothermal activity test results confirmed the excellent photogenerated ROS and photothermal properties of AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti under near-infrared laser irradiation. In vitro antibacterial and in vivo anti-infection experiments showed that the coating combined with photodynamic and photothermal therapies to eradicate bacteria and disrupt mature biofilms under 1064 nm laser irradiation. Consequently, AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti shows promise as an implant coating for treating implant-associated infections post-surgery, thereby enhancing the success rate of implantation procedures. This study also provides a new idea for combating implant-associated infections.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Anti-infective coating
Implant-associated infection
Photocatalytic therapy
Photothermal therapy
Antibacterial

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Construction of AgBiS2/Ti coating on titanium surface
Detection through electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy
Evaluation of the antibacterial and anti-biofilm properties
Characterization of composition and structure of AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti
Photocatalytic and photothermal activity of nanocomposite coatings
Antibacterial and anti-biofilm properties of nanocomposite coatings

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledged financial support from the Natural Science Key Foundation of Guangdong Province ( 2019B1515120056 ), National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51872124 ), the Innovation Team Project of Foshan City ( FS0AA-KJ919-4402-0086 ), and the open experimental project by GETRC of Graphene-like Materials and Products ( GETRCGL-202207 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fabrication of AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti nano-composite coatings. (A) Schematic of the preparation of AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti coating. SEM images of (B) AgBiS2/Ti and (C) AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti. HRTEM images of (D) AgBiS2 on the AgBiS2/Ti surface, (E) CQDs, and (F, G) AgBiS2@CQDs on the AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti coating surface.

Characterization of AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti. (A) XRD patterns and (B) FTIR spectrum of Ti, AgBiS2/Ti, CQDs, and AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti. (C) XPS spectrum of AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti. High-resolution spectra of (D) Ag 3d, (E) Bi 4f/S 2p, (F) C 1s, (G) N 1s, (H) and O 1s in AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti. (I) Water contact angles of the surfaces of Ti, Ti-OH, AgBiS2/Ti, and AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti.

Photodynamic and photothermal performance analysis of AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti. (A) Transient photocurrent density curves of Ti, AgBiS2/Ti, and AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti. (B) PL emission spectra of AgBiS2/Ti and AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti. (C) DCFH-DA fluorescence intensities of different samples after 10 min of NIR irradiation. (D) ROS level changes of AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti under NIR light irradiation for different times. (E) ·OH and (F) 1O2 production capacity of AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti detected by EPR. (G) Photothermal images and (H) the corresponding temperature changes of Ti, AgBiS2/Ti, and AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti. (I) The photothermal stability test of AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti.

In vitro antibacterial activity of AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti. (A) Changes in intracellular ROS levels of S. aureus and E. coli with and without illumination. (B) Antibacterial effect of different samples with and without NIR laser irradiation. Antibacterial rates of (C) S. aureus and (D) E. coli after different treatments. (E) Fluorescence image and (F) SEM image showing the damage of the bacterial cell membranes. Nucleic acid leakage levels of (G) S. aureus and (H) E. coli after different treatments. (I) Biofilm-disrupting properties of Ti, AgBiS2/Ti and AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti with and without NIR irradiation. (J) Survival state of bacteria within biofilms.

Biosafety testing of AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti. (A) In vitro hemocompatibility of H2O (I), PBS (II), Ti (III), AgBiS2/Ti (IV), and AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti (V). (B) Cytocompatibility of Ti, AgBiS2/Ti, and AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti. (C) The H&E-stained sections of the major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lungs, and kidneys) after Ti, AgBiS2/Ti, and AgBiS2@CQDs /Ti were implanted in mice. (D) Hepatic and (E) renal function indicators of the implanted mice. (F) Blood routine test of the implanted mice.

In vivo anti-infection testing of AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti. (A) Infrared thermal images of Ti and AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti under NIR laser irradiation and the corresponding (B) temperature change graphs. (C) Weight changes of mice in different treatment groups within 7 days after implantation surgery. (D) Number of surviving bacteria around the implant and (E) their antibacterial rate in different treatment groups. (F) Giemsa staining, (G) hematoxylin & eosin staining, and (H) IL-6 immunohistochemical staining of tissues around the implant.
Schematic diagram of the antibacterial and anti-biofilm mechanism of AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti composite nanocoatings under NIR laser irradiation.

head.ce:title: AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti nanocomposite coatings for combating implant-associated infections by photodynamic /photothermal therapy

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Engineering & Technology Research Centre of Graphene-like Materials and Products, Department of Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Jinan University, Guangzhou 510632, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Engineering & Technology Research Centre of Graphene-like Materials and Products
College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangdong Engineering & Technology Research Centre of Graphene-like Materials and Products, Department of Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Jinan University, Guangzhou 510632, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biofilm-mediated implant-associated infections are one of the most serious complications of implantation surgery, posing a grave threat to patient well-being. Effectively addressing bacterial infections is crucial for the success of implantation procedures. In this study, we prepared a bismuth sulfide silver@carbon quantum dot composite coating (AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti) on a medical titanium surface by surface engineering design to treat implant-associated infections. The photocatalytic/photothermal activity test results confirmed the excellent photogenerated ROS and photothermal properties of AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti under near-infrared laser irradiation. In vitro antibacterial and in vivo anti-infection experiments showed that the coating combined with photodynamic and photothermal therapies to eradicate bacteria and disrupt mature biofilms under 1064 nm laser irradiation. Consequently, AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti shows promise as an implant coating for treating implant-associated infections post-surgery, thereby enhancing the success rate of implantation procedures. This study also provides a new idea for combating implant-associated infections.

A bismuth sulphide silver@carbon quantum dots nanocomposite coating (AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti) was constructed on titanium substrates. The coating exhibited excellent photocatalytic activity and photothermal performance under 1064 nm near-infrared laser irradiation, which could generate ROS and localized high temperatures to kill bacteria and destroy biofilm, thus showing a good anti-infection effect in vivo.
Unlabelled Image

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Advancements in healthcare and the aspiration for improved quality of life have resulted in an expanding demand for surgical implantation procedures. Although healthcare professionals have tried their best to provide patients with a sterile surgical environment, the development of bacterial infections is still inevitable [1]. Statistically, the infection rate for various implants, such as fixation devices or prosthetic joints, ranges from 2% to 5% during initial implantation, with a significantly heightened risk of infection following revision surgery [2,3]. Moreover, the compromised local immune function at the surgical site further elevates the susceptibility to bacterial infection [4]. Consequently, high doses of antibiotics are often required post-implant surgery to combat invading pathogenic bacteria [5]. The formation of bacterial biofilms on implant surfaces not only diminishes the efficacy of antibiotic treatment but also contributes to bacterial resistance [6,7], ultimately exacerbating the infection. Furthermore, systemic administration of high-dose antibiotics may lead to serious biotoxicity [8]. Given these challenges, there is an urgent need to explore new therapeutic approaches to treat bacterial infections in implant surgery.

The engineering design of implant surfaces presents a promising avenue for addressing implant-associated infections (IAIs) [9,10]. Applying coatings to the surface of the implant offers a means to enhance its biological activities [11–13], like antibacterial properties and promotion of osteogenesis. For example, immobilizing glucose oxidase (GOD) on biomedical materials using tannic acid (TA) has shown promise for antibacterial and anti-biofilm purposes [14]. Recent advancements have introduced various antibacterial strategies tailored for precise treatment of implant coatings, including sonotherapy (acoustic-thermal and sonodynamic therapy) [15–17], phototherapy (photothermal and photodynamic therapy) [18–20], and magnetotherapy [21,22]. Phototherapy, in particular, is widely utilized in clinical settings due to its spatio-temporal controllability and tissue penetration capabilities. On the one hand, photothermal therapy (PTT) harnesses the photothermal effect of near-infrared (NIR) lasers on the coating surface to generate localized high temperatures, thereby facilitating antibacterial activity and biofilm destruction [23,24]. Xu et al. deposited black phosphorus (BP) onto a polydimethylsiloxane substrate (PDMS) and achieved effective biofilm disruption by photothermal [25]. On the other hand, photodynamic therapy (PDT) employs laser irradiation to transform H2O and O2 near the photosensitizer coating into harmful reactive oxygen species (ROS), inducing oxidative stress and bacterial eradication [26,27]. The combination therapy of PDT and PTT holds promise for addressing implant-associated infections. However, the depth of tissue penetration by NIR lasers is a critical factor, particularly for implants located in deep tissues [28]. Conventional near-infrared one-region lasers (NIRI, wavelength: 650–900 nm) are limited in their ability to penetrate deep tissues due to strong light scattering and tissue absorption [29]. In contrast, near-infrared two-region lasers (NIR-II, wavelength: 1000–1700 nm) exhibit reduced tissue absorption and greater tissue penetration depth, making them better suited for combating implant-associated infections [30].

AgBiS2, a semiconductor material with a narrow bandgap (≈1.2 eV), possesses favorable characteristics for photocatalytic applications because its light absorption range spans across the ultraviolet, visible, and near-infrared regions [31,32]. Additionally, AgBiS2 has shown promise for solar energy conversion and thermoelectric power generation [33]. In a previous study, our group reported on the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) by AgBiS2 in the presence of an 808 nm laser for bactericidal purposes and further elucidated the band gap of AgBiS2 and the underlying mechanism of photogenerated ROS [34]. Essentially, near-infrared (NIR) radiation stimulates valence band electrons in AgBiS2, causing them to transition to the conduction band, resulting in the creation of vacancies (or holes) in the valence band and the formation of energetically active electron-hole pairs. In biological tissues, the transfer of energy from these highly active electrons and holes to O2 and H2O leads to the generation of ROS, thereby implementing the bactericidal strategy of PDT [35,36]. Remarkably, the recombination process of photogenerated carriers during NIR irradiation of AgBiS2 will generate heat, contributing significantly to the photothermal effect [37]. However, the narrow band gap of AgBiS2 leads to an elevated rate of photogenerated electron-hole complexation [38], thereby imposing substantial constraints on its photocatalytic activity. Previous studies have demonstrated that the compounding of semiconductors with carbon-based materials, such as graphene, carbon quantum dots, carbon nanotubes, and fullerenes, can modify the bandgap of the semiconductors, enhancing the separation efficiency of photogenerated electrons and holes, consequently improving photocatalytic activity [39]. Among these carbon-based materials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are particularly suitable as charge-transfer bridges because of their small size, attributed to their exceptional electron transfer and storage capabilities, as well as their cost-effectiveness [40,41]. Therefore, the combination of AgBiS2 and CQDs could potentially serve as a viable approach to enhance the photocatalytic activity for combating implant-associated infections in deep tissues.

In this study, we constructed a nanocomposite coating (AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti) of bismuth silver sulfide and carbon quantum dots on a medical titanium substrate. When subjected to NIR laser irradiation at a wavelength of 1064 nm, AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti exhibits remarkable photocatalytic activity, effectively catalyzes the generation of hydroxyl radicals (·OH) and singlet oxygen (1O2) from water and oxygen in the vicinity of the coating. Meanwhile, the AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti shows exceptional photothermal effects under NIR irradiation. Under the synergistic effect of PDT and PTT, the composite coating up-regulated the ROS level in bacterial cells and elevated the temperature near the implant, realizing the eradication of bacteria and the rapid destruction of mature biofilm (Scheme 1 ). In vivo experiments demonstrated that the AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti composite could eradicate pathogenic bacteria under NIR laser irradiation, which significantly suppressed the inflammatory response caused by bacterial infection. Therefore, we hope this study can offer an antibiotic-free therapeutic approach for addressing infections associated with medical implants, while also introducing a novel concept for the development of coatings for implantable medical devices.

Silver chloride (AgCl, AR), bismuth chloride (BiCl3, AR) and thiourea (CH4N2S, AR) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co. Citric acid monohydrate (C6H8O7·H2O, AR) was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Co. Anhydrous ethylenediamine (C2H8N2, AR), anhydrous ethanol (C2H6O, AR), acetone (C3H6O, AR) and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, AR) were purchased from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory. Escherichia coli (ATCC 8739) and Staphylococcus aureus (ATCC 6538) were purchased from the Guangdong Institute of Microbiology.

The 10 × 10 × 1 mm titanium plates (purchased from Haiyuan Aluminum Co., Ltd.) were polished using silicon carbide sandpaper (#400, #600, #800, and #1000). The titanium plates were ultrasonically cleaned with acetone, ethanol, and deionized water for 10 min to remove the surface dirt. To enhance the hydrophilicity of the polished titanium plates, they were soaked in a 30 mL of NaOH (1 M) solution for 20 h at room temperature and then washed with deionized water and dried.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of AgBiS2/Ti coating on titanium surface
ce:section.ce:para: The AgBiS2 NRs coatings were synthesized on the Ti surface following the method of Wu et al. [42]. The AgBiS2 precursor solution was prepared by dissolving 4 mmol of AgCl, 4 mmol of BiCl3, and 16 mmol of thiourea in 5 mL of DMSO and stirring at room temperature until dissolution, and then filtered through a 0.22 μm filter. Subsequently, 10 μL of AgBiS2 precursor solution was dispensed and deposited onto the surface of alkali-treated titanium plates, which were uniformly coated by rotational application in a homogenizer at a speed of 4000 rpm for 20 s. The coated plates were then transferred to a heated container at 300 °C and kept for 3 min. This process of rotational coating and in situ thermal growth was repeated eight times. Finally, the samples were annealed in a heating plate at 400 °C for 10 min. Both thermal growth and annealing processes were carried out in the N2 atmosphere. The specific formation mechanism can be expressed as follows:
AgCl + TU → Ag-TU (TU = thiourea)
BiCl3 + TU → Bi-TU
Ag-TU + Bi-TU → AgBiS2

In the precursor solution, the formation of complexes (Ag-thiourea and Bi-thiourea) prevents the formation of Ag2S and Bi2S3 so that Ag-thiourea and Bi-thiourea can be oriented to produce AgBiS2 nanorods by undergoing thermal decomposition at the reaction temperature.

5 mmol of citric acid and 335 μL of ethylenediamine were dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water, and then the solution was then transferred into a 50 mL Teflon liner and heated in a reactor at 200 °C for 5 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was filtered using a 0.22 μm filter. The filtrate was then loaded into a dialysis bag with a molecular weight of 500 MW and dialyzed in deionized water for 24 h. The deionized water was changed three times during this period. Finally, the sample underwent a freeze-drying process lasting 36 h to obtain CQDs.

A small quantity of CQDs was introduced into 50 mL of deionized water and subjected to ultrasonication for 20 min to achieve uniform dispersion. Subsequently, the titanium plate loaded with AgBiS2 and the aforementioned solution was introduced into a round-bottomed flask with a capacity of 100 mL. The flask was then heated in a water bath at 90 °C for 30 min. Finally, the AgBiS2/Ti substrate, loaded with CQDs, was removed from the solution. The CQDs not well adhered to the surface were washed off using deionized water and dried in an oven at 60 °C for 1 h. This process resulted in the formation of the AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti nanocomposite coating.

A three-electrode system was established, with the samples (Ti, AgBiS2/Ti, and AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti) serving as the working electrode, a calomel electrode as the reference electrode, and a platinum electrode as the counter electrode. Subsequently, the photoelectrochemical properties of the samples were tested using an electrochemical workstation in a 1 mol/L Na2SO4 aqueous solution. In addition, the emission wavelengths of AgBiS2/Ti and AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti were measured using a fluorescence spectrometer.

The ROS generation in various samples was measured in the presence of an NIR laser using the 2,7-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) probe. First, Ti, AgBiS2/Ti, and AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti were immersed in a 2 mL solution of DCFH-DA at a concentration of 20 μM. Subsequently, the samples were exposed to NIR radiation for 10 min. An enzyme marker then detected the fluorescence intensity of the solutions. Additionally, the change of fluorescence intensity of AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti was measured under different illumination times (0, 2, 4, 7, and 10 min).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection through electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: An aqueous solution containing the trapping agent 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) was applied to the surface of the samples. The samples were then exposed to NIR radiation for 10 min. Following this, the ·OH signal was determined by EPR spectroscopy.

Similarly, a solution of DMSO containing the trapping agent 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine oxide (TEMPO) was applied to the surface of the sample. The sample was then exposed to NIR radiation for 10 min. Subsequently, the 1O2 signal was determined by EPR spectroscopy.

Ti, AgBiS2/Ti, and AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti specimens were placed in a laser confocal dish containing 1 mL of PBS, and the samples were irradiated using a 1064 nm laser (1.0 W/cm2). An infrared thermography camera was employed to monitor the changes in sample temperature and capture thermography images.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the antibacterial and anti-biofilm properties

ce:section.ce:para: The in vitro antibacterial performance of the composite coating was assessed by employing Escherichia coli (E. coli, Gram-negative bacterium) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus, Gram-positive bacterium) as bacterial models. Firstly, the samples were sterilized by irradiation using a UV germicidal lamp for 1 h. Subsequently, the samples were divided into a dark group (no NIR irradiation) and an NIR group. The samples were then immersed in 1 mL of bacterial suspension (~1 × 108 CFU/mL), with the NIR group receiving NIR laser treatment (1064 nm, 1.0 W/cm2) for 10 min. Following this, the bacterial suspension was dispersed and evenly coated in Luria-Bertani solid medium, and the samples were then incubated at 37 °C for 16 h.

Bacteria from various treatments were mixed with a SYTO 9/PI fluorescent staining solution and incubated for 1 h in darkness. Afterward, the bacteria were collected, and the cell fluorescence was observed by a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM).

Titanium plates with bacteria attached were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde solution overnight. The plates were then dehydrated using a series of ethanol concentrations (30%, 50%, 70%, 90%, and 100%). The resulting bacterial morphology following the different treatments was subsequently examined using scanning electron microscopy.

The samples were incubated with bacterial suspensions (~1 × 108 CFU/mL) in the log phase for 3 days at 37 °C to form mature biofilms on the sample surface. Next, the samples in the NIR group received a 10-min NIR laser irradiation. The biofilms were then stained using a SYTO 9/PI fluorescent staining solution, and their three-dimensional structures were observed under a confocal laser scanning microscope.

Fresh New Zealand rabbit blood erythrocytes (4%, V/V) were centrifuged at 2500 rpm/min for 5 min, then diluted to 1% with PBS solution. Subsequently, 1 mL of the diluted erythrocyte solution was transferred to a 24-well culture plate. The samples were gently introduced into the erythrocyte solution and incubated statically for 4 h in a 37 °C incubator. After co-incubation, the supernatant was centrifuged to determine the OD540 nm, and the hemolysis rate was calculated according to the formula. Deionized water and PBS were added to the erythrocyte solution as positive and negative controls, respectively. The formula for calculating the hemolysis rate is as follows:
Hemolysis Rate =
OD sample
−
OD negative
OD positive
−
OD negative
× 100 %
ODsample is the absorbance of the sample group, and ODpositive and ODnegative represent the absorbance of the positive control and negative control, respectively.

We employed mouse embryonic fibroblasts (NIH/3T3 cells) as representative cells. NIH/3T3 cells (cell number ~ 106) were inoculated in confocal dishes and cultured in a 37 °C incubator for 12 h to facilitate cell adhesion. Subsequently, the sterilized Ti implants from each group were slightly placed into the confocal dish and further incubate for 3 h in a 37 °C incubator. After the incubation, we carefully removed the implants and stained the cells with SYTO 9/PI fluorescent probe. Finally, the staining of the cells was observed by CLSM.

The samples were implanted into the subcutaneous tissue of the back of mice using the same method as in the in vivo anti-infection experiments. Fourteen days later, the mice were euthanized, and their major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lungs, and kidneys) were extracted for histopathological studies. Meanwhile, peripheral blood and serum samples were collected from the mice for routine blood analysis and liver and kidney function tests, respectively.

All animal experiments followed the Regulations on Animal Management of the Ministry of Health of the People's Republic of China (No. 55, 2001) and the Guidelines for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals of Jinan University (Guangdong).

Eight-week-old female Balb/c mice were selected for the experiments. The mice were randomly divided into 6 groups (n = 5): Ti group, Ti + NIR group, AgBiS2/Ti group, AgBiS2/Ti + NIR group, AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti group, and AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti + NIR group. The mice were anesthetized with pentobarbital sodium injection (1.5%) and depilated on the dorsal area using a razor and depilatory cream. Next, the dorsal region was sterilized with medical alcohol, and an10 mm incision was made on their backs using sterile medical scissors. The implants were then submerged in a suspension of S. aureus at a concentration of 1 × 108 CFU/mL for 30 min. Subsequently, they were extracted and subcutaneously implanted into the mice, with the wound being closed using a 4-O surgical suture. The mice in the NIR group received 10 min of NIR irradiation the following day, and localized thermographic images were captured. After 7 days, the mice were euthanized, the titanium implants were extracted, and the number of bacteria near the implants was assessed. Finally, the tissues surrounding the implant were collected for histological analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of composition and structure of AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti
ce:section.ce:para: The design and synthesis of the anti-infective coating is shown in Fig. 1A. Briefly, bismuth sulfide silver nano-coatings were constructed on medical titanium surfaces using spin coating and in-situ thermal generation [42]. Subsequently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were loaded onto AgBiS2 surfaces to form AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti nanocomposite coatings. To enhance the adhesion of the coating on the substrate surface, we surface-modified the Ti substrate before preparing the coating. As shown in Fig. S1, the Ti surface was initially smooth, while a multi-hollow network structure was formed on the Ti surface after the modification (Ti-OH), significantly enhancing the adhesion between the coating and the substrate. The surface morphology of the AgBiS2/Ti coating and AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti composite coating was observed with SEM. AgBiS2 exhibits a pine needle-like nanorod structure on the titanium substrate, with nanorods having diameters up to 200 nm and heights ranging from 200 nm to 500 nm (Fig. 1B). Compared to the zero-dimensional structure of nanoparticles, two-dimensional AgBiS2 nanorods have lower surface energy barriers and higher carrier mobility rates [43], indicating potential for photocatalytic reactive oxygen species generation. There was no significant difference in the surface between AgBiS2/Ti and the sample modified by CQDs (Fig. 1C), possibly because the small particle size of CQDs has little effect on the surface morphology of AgBiS2. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was further used to characterize the surface morphology and microstructure of the samples. The TEM image in Fig. 1D clearly showed that AgBiS2 had a needle-like nanorod morphology, consistent with the results observed by SEM. Meanwhile, the small-size structure of CQDs can be seen in Fig. 1E, with a size of less than 10 nm. The small size of CQDs allows them to make full contact with the surface of AgBiS2 nanorods, facilitating the transfer of photogenerated carriers in AgBiS2 and enhancing carrier migration efficiency. Fig. 1F showed that CQDs in AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti were well composited with AgBiS2. Importantly, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) revealed that the lattice stripe spacing of 2.8 Å and 2.3 Å corresponded well with the (200) crystallographic plane of AgBiS2 and the (100) crystallographic plane of CQDs (Fig. 1G), indicating that CQDs were successfully loaded onto the surface of AgBiS2 to form the AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti nanocomposite coating. We also examined the bonding strength between the substrate and the coatings, AgBiS2/Ti and AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti. Specifically, samples were fixed to stainless steel rods using an instantaneous adhesive and tested for coating-substrate bond strength using a universal testing machine, as we previously reported [44]. As shown in Fig. S2, the bond strength of AgBiS2/Ti reached 13.89 ± 1.41 MPa, while that of AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti was 19.39 ± 2.62 MPa, indicating that the composite nano-coatings have good mechanical stability. Moreover, we immersed AgBiS2/Ti and AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti in PBS buffer solution and saline and subsequently detected their metal ion content seven days later to confirm their chemical stability. The results showed that after 7 days of immersion, no significant precipitation of silver ions was detected in both PBS buffer solution and saline (Fig. S3), indicating that the composite coatings have excellent chemical stability.

We conducted a comprehensive analysis of the chemical structure of the composite coatings. The X-ray diffraction patterns showed characteristic peaks at 27.06°, 31.45°, 45.13°, 53.62°, and 56.21° for the as-prepared AgBiS2/Ti (Fig. 2A), corresponding to the (111), (200), (220), (311), and (222) crystallographic facets of AgBiS2 [45]. CQDs exhibit a broad peak at 25° corresponding to the (002) crystallographic plane [46]. Interestingly, the AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti samples showed no distinct peaks associated with CQDs, possibly attributed to the relatively low content of CQDs in the AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti samples, consistent with previous findings [41,47]. Subsequently, the functional groups and chemical compositions of the composite coatings were analyzed via Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The FTIR spectrum revealed the presence of characteristic vibrational peaks of AgS and BiS at 482.8 cm−1 and 609 cm−1 for AgBiS2/Ti; the sharp band located at 1075 cm−1 belonged to the SO stretching vibrational peaks [48] (Fig. 2B). In addition, the CQDs exhibited a broad peak at 3260 cm−1 and 2900 cm−1, which can be ascribed to the stretching vibrations of OH and methylene or methyl (CH); the peaks observed at 1651 cm−1, 1520 cm−1, and 1370 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of CO, NH, and C-O-C, respectively [49]. The presence of these functional groups suggested the excellent hydrophilicity of the synthesized CQDs. Moreover, the FTIR spectrum of the AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti sample exhibited simultaneous absorption peaks corresponding to the functional groups of AgBiS2 and CQDs, indicating the successful composite formation of AgBiS2 and CQDs. The full-scan XPS spectra revealed that the AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti composite coatings mainly consisted of silver (Ag), bismuth (Bi), sulfur (S), carbon (C), nitrogen (N), and oxygen (O) (Fig. 2C). The double peaks (373.7 eV and 367.3 eV) demonstrated in the high-resolution XPS spectra of Ag 3d can be ascribed to Ag 3d3/2 and Ag 3d5/2 of Ag+ [35] (Fig. 2D). The XPS high-resolution spectra of Bi 4f and S 2p in the samples are presented in Fig. 2E. Bi 4f has a well-separated spin-orbit component located at 157.8 eV (4f7/2) and 163.1 eV (4f5/2), respectively, which corresponds to the lattice Bi characteristic binding energies of the +3-valent oxidation state of AgBiS2 [34]. Importantly, a pair of satellite peaks (158.9 eV and 164.2 eV) belonging to the antisite defect of Bi was also observed at relatively high binding energies, suggesting that it can be used as a nonradiative combining site for inducing photothermal effects in AgBiS2. Additionally, overlapping with the Bi 4f spectra, small peaks at 161.9 eV and 160.7 eV matching S 2p1/2 and S 2p3/2, respectively, with a distance of 1.2 eV between the two orbitals of the splitting, were observed. These structures confirm the successful preparation of AgBiS2 [50]. Besides, the C 1s high-resolution spectrum had three peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.5 eV, and 288.0 eV (Fig. 2F), which were attributed to C-C/C=C, CN, and CO, respectively [51]. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2G) exhibited two distinct peaks at 399.0 eV and 400.4 eV, which can be attributed to the NH and C-N-C of the CQDs, respectively. There were three peaks at 530.4 eV, 531.5 eV, and 532.2 eV in the O 1s high-resolution spectrum (Fig. 2H), which were attributed to CO, COOH, and C-O-C/-OH, respectively [52]. These results showed that the surface of CQDs was enriched with many oxygen-containing functional groups, implying that the composite coating had excellent hydrophilicity. Next, we examined the surface hydrophilicity of the samples, and the findings showed that AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti had the smallest water contact angle (Fig. 2I). This result demonstrated that AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti had good hydrophilicity and better biocompatibility. Therefore, the above experimental results confirmed the successful preparation of the AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti nanocomposite coating.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic and photothermal activity of nanocomposite coatings
ce:section.ce:para: Next, we tested the photocatalytic activity of AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti composite coatings. Firstly, the charge separation efficiencies of Ti, AgBiS2/Ti, and AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti were compared under NIR light irradiation by testing photocurrents. As shown in Fig. 3A, during the experiment involving 9 switching cycles of intermittent NIR light, it was observed that Ti exhibited minimal photocurrent response. Conversely, AgBiS2/Ti and AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti displayed a significant photocurrent response. The comparison showed that the photocurrent response value of AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti was approximately three times higher than that of AgBiS2/Ti. The primary factor contributing to this phenomenon is the narrow bandgap of AgBiS2, which facilitates the complexation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. It is worth noting that the composite coating has superior photocatalytic performance due to the exceptional electron conductivity of CQDs, which effectively improves the photogenerated electron-hole separation efficiency of AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti. Meanwhile, the current value of the photo-response remains relatively constant and shows no sign of weakening, thereby verifying the exceptional rate of photogenerated carrier separation in the samples. Subsequently, a comparison was made between the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of AgBiS2/Ti and AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti. Photoluminescence (PL) is a feasible tool to characterize the efficiency of photogenerated carrier separation and complexation in photocatalysts, as it is mainly generated by the complexation of photogenerated electrons (e−) and photogenerated holes (h+) [53]. Therefore, higher peak intensities reflect higher photogenerated e− and h+ complexation rates, meaning lower photogenerated carrier separation efficiency. Fig. 3B shows that the fluorescence intensity of AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti is significantly lower than that of AgBiS2/Ti, suggesting that the AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti composite coatings have a much higher photogenerated electron-hole separation efficiency, and thus perform superior NIR photocatalytic performance. To assess the photocatalytic efficacy of the samples, DCFH-DA was used as a probe to detect the ROS generation level after NIR light exposure. The results indicated that the fluorescence intensity of the AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti group was much higher than that of the other sample groups after 10 min of NIR light irradiation (Fig. 3C). Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity rapidly increased with the extension of NIR illumination time (Fig. 3D), demonstrating that the photogenerated ROS activity of the composite coatings showed a time-dependence. We also examined the photogenerated ROS performance of the composite coatings by electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy. Thanks to the enhanced photocatalytic activity of AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti, the generation of hydroxyl radical (·OH) and singlet oxygen (1O2) was significantly facilitated under the NIR laser irradiation (Fig. 3E and Fig. 3F). The test results implied the capability of AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti to generate ROS to eradicate bacteria when exposed to NIR laser.

Furthermore, we examined the photothermal properties of Ti, AgBiS2/Ti, and AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti. The temperature changes of the samples during a 10-min exposure to a 1064 nm laser were monitored using an infrared thermography camera. Fig. 3G present the thermal images corresponding to the surface temperatures of the samples at various time intervals (0, 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10 min) during irradiation. All samples underwent rapid heating within 1 min of irradiation. The temperature reached 43 °C for pure Ti, while AgBiS2/Ti and AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti exhibited similar heating profiles, with temperatures around 52 °C. Eventually, after 10 min of irradiation, the temperature of AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti increased to 62 °C (Fig. 3H), indicating the excellent photothermal conversion performance of the composite coating. Notably, the elevated temperature would result in the denaturation of bacterial proteins and the disruption of bacterial membrane, causing damage to the bacteria and ultimately leading to their inactivation. The cyclic photothermal diagrams showed that the photothermal effect of AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti remained constant even after three cycles (Fig. 3I), suggesting that the coating exhibited stable photothermal performance. In order to further study the photothermal performance of AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti, we calculated the photothermal conversion efficiency of AgBiS2@CQDs. The calculation results showed that the photothermal conversion efficiency of AgBiS2@CQDs was 41.6% (Fig. S4, Fig. S5, and Fig. S6), confirming its excellent photothermal conversion performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial and anti-biofilm properties of nanocomposite coatings
ce:section.ce:para: Inspired by the remarkable photocatalytic activity and photothermal properties exhibited by AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti composite coating, we further investigated its in vitro antibacterial performance. As shown in Fig. 4A, the AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti present a notable ability to enhance the intracellular ROS level of bacteria under near-infrared laser illumination, indicating the ability of the coating to effectively damage bacteria under the near-infrared laser. Notably, the intensity of green fluorescence produced in the AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti group was considerably higher compared to that of AgBiS2/Ti (Fig. S7), suggesting that the combination of AgBiS2 and CQDs can substantially enhance the ROS yield and promote oxidative damage to bacteria. The survival status of the bacteria was assessed using the dilution plate coating method. The experimental results (Fig. 4B) showed that the antibacterial activities of AgBiS2/Ti and AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti samples (except pure Ti) significantly increased after 10 min of NIR laser irradiation, with AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti demonstrating the highest antibacterial activity. Calculations revealed that AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti exhibited a remarkable antibacterial efficacy of 92.7% and 91.1% against S. aureus and E. coli, respectively (Fig. 4C and Fig. 4D). We further employed the live/dead bacterial staining method to detect the integrity of bacterial cell membranes. Under dark conditions, there was strong green fluorescence in the bacteria co-incubated with the samples (Ti, AgBiS2/Ti, and AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti), indicating that the cell membrane integrity of the bacteria was not disrupted (Fig. 4E). It means AgBiS2/Ti and AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti have no antibacterial activity without the NIR laser. However, significant red fluorescence was displayed in bacteria co-incubated with AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti when exposed to NIR laser, suggesting that the integrity of the bacterial membrane was severely damaged. Furthermore, SEM observations of S. aureus and E. coli on Ti, AgBiS2/Ti, and AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti samples revealed that under NIR conditions, the cells in the AgBiS2/Ti and AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti groups showed different degrees of indentation, distortion, and breakage, accompanied by the leakage of contents, confirming that the bacteria perished as a result of compromised cell membrane integrity (Fig. 4F). This finding is consistent with the conclusions drawn from the live/dead bacterial staining experiments. Moreover, the AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti group exhibited the highest amount of bacterial nucleic acid leakage, further confirming its superior antibacterial performance (Fig. 4G and Fig. 4H). These experimental results showed that the effective catalytic activity of AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti, in promoting the reaction of water and oxygen around the coating to generate ROS and disrupt the integrity of the bacterial cell membrane, is due to its superior electron-hole pair separation efficiency. Meanwhile, the exceptional photothermal conversion properties of AgBiS2@CQDs contribute to the denaturation of bacterial proteins, thereby improving the antibacterial efficacy of the composite coating. Furthermore, we conducted a comparison between monotherapy using H2O2 + Fe2+ (ROS) and a 60 °C water bath (heat treatment) and synergistic therapy. As shown in Fig. S8, while single ROS and single heat treatment exhibit some antibacterial activity, their efficiencies are insufficient to eradicate biofilm-released bacteria in vivo. In contrast, AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti combined PDT and PTT under NIR laser irradiation, resulting in significantly improved bacteria-killing capabilities. Therefore, through the synergistic effect of PDT and PTT, AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti can rapidly kill the bacteria and show remarkable antibacterial performance.

We conducted additional investigations to assess the efficacy of the composite coating in disrupting biofilms. First, the samples were placed in a bacterial culture during the logarithmic growth phase and then subjected to a stationary incubation period of 3 days. Following this, the samples in the light group were subjected to irradiation for 10 min using a NIR laser. We utilized a 3D confocal laser scanning microscope to examine the state of the bacterial biofilm (Fig. 4I). In the treatment group without NIR laser exposure, patches of intact green fluorescence indicated the viability and integrity of the mature bacterial biofilm. Conversely, following NIR laser irradiation, the red signal on the surface of AgBiS2/Ti and AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti was notably intensified, suggesting varying degrees of damage to the established biofilm. Subsequently, we further examined the survival rate of bacteria within the biofilm after the various treatments. The results showed that the AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti + NIR group exhibited survival rates of only 3.1% for S. aureus and 2.6% for E. coli (Fig. 4J), demonstrating that the combination of high temperature and ROS successfully damaged the biofilm and rapidly eradicated the bacteria residing within it.

As a long-term implantable antibacterial biomaterial, the biosafety of the surface coating during implantation is a crucial consideration, besides the excellent antibacterial and anti-biofilm properties. First, we tested the blood compatibility of the samples in accordance with ISO 10993-4 [54] (Fig. 5A). The results showed that no obvious hemolysis in the red blood cells exposed to Ti, AgBiS2/Ti, and AgBiS2@CQDS/Ti, with the hemolysis rates below 5%, demonstrating favorable blood compatibility. Consequently, they can effectively mitigate the occurrence of severe systemic adverse reactions associated with the coatings in practical applications. Additionally, an investigation was conducted on the cellular biotoxicity of the composite coating. After incubating NIH/3T3 cells with the respective samples, we observed no adverse effects on cell viability, indicating a significant level of cytocompatibility (Fig. 5B). Subsequently, the samples were implanted into mice to evaluate their biosafety in vivo. Using a random allocation method, we divided the mice into four groups and implanted Ti, AgBiS2/Ti, and AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti into the subcutaneous tissues of the mice, respectively, while the control group received no treatment. Importantly, no discernible pathological changes were detected in the H&E-stained sections of the major organs (Fig. 5C). In addition, the indicators of hepatic and renal functions (Fig. 5D and Fig. 5E), as well as the routine blood tests (Fig. 5F) of the mice, were within the normal range, suggesting a high degree of biosafety for the samples used in the experiments. Subsequently, we also verified the biosafety of AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti under NIR laser irradiation through in vitro and in vivo experiments. In the in vitro experiments using the CCK-8 method, we found that the high temperature generated by NIR laser irradiation did lead to the death of a small number of NIH/3T3 cells. However, the survival rate of the cells after NIR laser irradiation was significantly increased after culturing for 48 h in a 37 °C incubator (Fig. S9). This increase was mainly due to the continuous proliferation of the surviving cells, effectively replenishing their numbers. The results showed that the skin of the mice after NIR laser irradiation did not exhibit necrosis due to the high temperature, as the temperature exceeded 55 °C for only a very small portion of the irradiation process, resulting in no significant damage to the surrounding tissues around the implant in the mice (Fig. S10). Therefore, we conclude that AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti does not cause damage to normal tissues during NIR laser treatment and can be further used for in vivo anti-infection testing.

Based on the outstanding antibacterial/anti-biofilm properties and favorable biosafety, we further conducted an in vivo study to evaluate the anti-infective effect of AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti nanocomposite coatings in mice. Briefly, Balb/c mice were randomly divided into six groups (n = 5): the Ti group, the Ti + NIR group, the AgBiS2/Ti group, the AgBiS2/Ti + NIR group, the AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti group, and the AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti + NIR group. Then, sterilized Ti, AgBiS2/Ti, and AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti were immersed in a bacterial solution of S. aureus for 30 min and implanted subcutaneously into the mice to establish an implant-associated infection model simulating bacterial infections resulting from the implantation procedure. Twenty-four hours later, 10 min of 1064 nm laser treatment was applied to mice in the Ti + NIR group, AgBiS2/Ti + NIR group, and AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti + NIR group. We first examined the photothermal properties of the AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti composite coating in vivo. Thermal imaging showed a progressive temperature increase at the light-exposed site of the mice in the AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti implantation group over time (Fig. 6A). Furthermore, the photothermal performance of this group was significantly superior to that of the Ti implantation group. Within ten minutes, the localized temperature around this coating could reach 58 °C (Fig. 6B), indicating that AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti maintains good photothermal performance in vivo. Significantly, the exceptional photothermal performance was primarily attributed to the favorable photothermal conversion properties of AgBiS2 and CQDs, as well as the greater tissue penetration depth of the 1064 nm laser. Next, we further investigated the anti-infection efficacy of the composite coating. Severe diseases often result in sudden weight loss, making weight change a crucial indicator of disease severity. As shown in Fig. 6C, one day after implanting the infected implant into the mice, a significant reduction in body weight was observed in all mice. At this time, the mice had elevated body temperatures and showed symptoms of fever, including loss of appetite, a dull gaze, and slow movement, demonstrating the severity of the bacterial infection in the mice. However, after NIR laser treatment, the mice gradually regained weight, normalized their body temperature, became more agile, and normalized their appetite. Among the experimental groups, the mice in the AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti + NIR group had the highest rate of weight increase due to the superior photocatalytic activity of AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti. In comparison, the mice in the group without NIR laser treatment showed slow weight gain, suggesting persistent infection. Seven days later, the mice were euthanized, and the remaining bacteria in the tissue around the implant were examined. The results showed that the bacterial inhibition rate achieved by AgBiS2@CQDs + NIR exceeded 99.3%, significantly higher than that of the other treatment groups (Fig. 6D and Fig. 6E). Moreover, dense bacterial colonies were observed in the Ti, AgBiS2/Ti, and AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti groups, confirming ineffective elimination of the infection. Subsequently, the peri-implant tissues in mice were observed by Giemsa staining (Fig. 6F). Except for the AgBiS2/Ti + NIR group and the AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti + NIR group, clumps of bacterial biofilms could be observed in all other groups, indicating the persistence of severe bacterial infections. In hematoxylin & eosin-stained sections, we observed that the AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti + NIR group had the most favorable histopathological outcomes. The tissues surrounding the implant were well-aligned, and no inflammatory cells were detected (Fig. 6G), suggesting that the infection was successfully controlled. In contrast, large numbers of neutrophils and lymphocytes could be observed in all other treatment groups, indicating that the infection-induced inflammatory response persisted in the peri-implant tissues. The presence of infection in the tissues was confirmed by performing immunohistochemical staining for interleukin-6 (IL-6) (Fig. 6H). The results showed the lowest IL-6 expression in the AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti + NIR group, demonstrating that AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti exhibited significant antibacterial properties under the effect of the NIR laser, which effectively suppressed the inflammatory response caused by infection. Overall, the AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti can effectively kill foreign pathogenic bacteria under NIR laser irradiation due to its excellent photogenerated ROS activity and photothermal properties, thereby effectively inhibiting bacterial infection and the associated inflammatory response.

In summary, we have successfully synthesized an AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti nanocomposite coating to combat implant-associated infections. Compared with AgBiS2/Ti, AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti exhibited superior photocatalytic activity under NIR laser irradiation, attributed to its higher separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, which catalyze the production of ·OH and 1O2 from water and oxygen. In addition, the coating exhibited excellent photothermal effect under NIR irradiation. Through the synergistic effect of PDT and PTT, AgBiS2@CQDs/Ti would kill bacteria and rapidly disrupt mature biofilms, thus providing an efficient means of inhibiting implant-associated infections. This photo-responsive multifunctional coating represents a new way to combat implant-associated infections mediated by biofilms.

Dongliang Huo: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Software, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Ting Liu: Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Software, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Kangkang Huang: Formal analysis, Methodology, Software, Validation. Changhui Que: Investigation, Methodology. Shuoyan Jiang: Investigation, Resources. Yuxia Yang: Investigation. Shaozao Tan: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Resources, Supervision. Langhuan Huang: Data curation, Investigation, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledged financial support from the Natural Science Key Foundation of Guangdong Province (2019B1515120056), National Natural Science Foundation of China (51872124), the Innovation Team Project of Foshan City (FS0AA-KJ919-4402-0086), and the open experimental project by GETRC of Graphene-like Materials and Products (GETRCGL-202207).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bioadv.2024.213763.
